questions.question: What is a P problem?
options: A) A decision tree with a polynomial time complexity
B) A decision tree with an exponential time complexity
C) A decision tree with an NP-complete time complexity
D) A decision tree with an unknown time complexity
questions.explanation: A P problem is a decision tree that can be solved in polynomial time. Examples include traveling salesman problem and knapsack problem.

questions.question: What does NP-Complete mean?
options: A) A set of problems that can be solved in polynomial time
B) A set of problems that can be solved in exponential time
C) A set of problems that are at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP
D) A set of problems that are always solvable in a finite number of steps
questions.explanation: NP-Complete problems are a set of problems that are at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP, meaning that solving one NP-Complete problem can help solve other NP problems.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of a decision tree with NP-complete time complexity?
options: A) Polynomial time
B) Exponential time
C) Linear time
D) Quadratic time
questions.explanation: A decision tree with NP-complete time complexity has an exponential time complexity, meaning that the time taken to solve the problem grows exponentially with the size of the input.

questions.question: What is the relationship between P and NP problems?
options: A) P problems are easier than NP problems
B) P problems are harder than NP problems
C) P problems and NP problems are equivalent
D) P problems are a subset of NP problems
questions.explanation: P problems are a subset of NP problems, meaning that all P problems can be solved in polynomial time, but not all NP problems can be solved in polynomial time.

questions.question: What is the significance of the Cook-Levin theorem?
options: A) It proved that P=NP
B) It proved that P≠NP
C) It proved that NP-complete problems are in NP
D) It proved that NP-complete problems are not in NP
questions.explanation: The Cook-Levin theorem proved that NP-complete problems are in NP, meaning that if a problem can be verified in polynomial time, then it can be solved in exponential time.
